1.1: Yahweh's word came to Jonah ben-Amittai:
1.2: 'Get up and go to Nineveh—that great city—and cry out against it. Their wickedness has come up before my face.'
1.3: Jonah bolted in the opposite direction—toward Tarshish, as far from Yahweh's face as a ship could carry him. He went down to Joppa, found a vessel heading for Tarshish, paid his passage, and went down into it—heading away, away from the presence of Yahweh.
1.4: Then Yahweh hurled a great wind onto the sea. The storm grew so fierce the ship groaned—on the verge of breaking apart.
1.5: The sailors were seized with panic—each crying to his own god, heaving the cargo into the sea to lighten the load. Meanwhile Jonah had gone below into the ship's hold and was lying there in a deep sleep.
1.6: The captain found him and said, 'How can you sleep at a time like this? Get up and pray to your God! Maybe he will notice us and we won't perish.'
1.7: The men said to one another, 'Let's cast lots and find out who has brought this trouble on us.' They cast the lots—and the lot fell on Jonah.
1.8: 'Tell us who's caused this,' they demanded. 'What is your trade? Where are you from? What nation do you belong to?'
1.9: 'I am a Hebrew,' he said. 'I worship Yahweh—the God of heaven—the one who made the sea and the dry land.'
1.10: The men were seized with terror. 'What have you done?' they said. They already knew he was running from Yahweh—he had told them himself.
1.11: 'What do we do with you to stop this sea?' they asked. The storm was growing worse by the moment.
1.12: 'Pick me up and throw me into the sea,' Jonah told them, 'and it will go calm. I know this violent storm has come because of me.'
1.13: The sailors tried to row for shore—they didn't want his blood on their hands—but the sea heaved against them and they made no headway.
1.14: So they cried out to Yahweh: 'LORD, please—do not let us perish for this man's life. Do not hold innocent blood against us. You, Yahweh, have done what you willed.'
1.15: They lifted Jonah and threw him into the sea—and at once the raging stopped.
1.16: The sailors were filled with awe before Yahweh. They offered him a sacrifice and made vows to him.
1.17: Yahweh had appointed a great fish to swallow Jonah—and Jonah was in the fish's belly three days and three nights.

2.1: From inside the fish's belly, Jonah prayed to Yahweh his God.
2.2:
He said:
In my anguish I called to Yahweh—
and he answered me.
From the depths of Sheol I cried out,
and you heard my voice.
2.3:
You threw me into the deep—
into the heart of the seas;
the current swept around me,
all your waves and breakers crashing over me.
2.4:
I thought: I am cut off from your sight.
Yet I will look again
toward your holy temple.
2.6:
I sank to the roots of the mountains;
the gates of the earth closed behind me—
a prison forever.
But you, Yahweh my God, brought my life up from the pit.
2.7:
When my breath was fading,
I remembered Yahweh—
and my prayer reached you,
into your holy temple.
2.8:
Those who cling to hollow idols
walk away from the mercy that could have been theirs.
2.10: Yahweh spoke to the fish—and it heaved Jonah up onto dry ground.

3.1: Yahweh's word came to Jonah a second time:
3.2: 'Get up. Go to Nineveh—that great city—and declare the message I will give you.'
3.3: Jonah got up and went to Nineveh, just as Yahweh had said. Nineveh was a vast city—three days' journey across.
3.4: After one day's walk into the city, Jonah proclaimed: 'Forty days—and Nineveh is finished!'
3.5: The Ninevites believed God. They proclaimed a fast and put on sackcloth—every person, from the greatest to the least.
3.6: Word reached the king of Nineveh. He rose from his throne, stripped off his royal robe, put on sackcloth, and sat in the ash heap.
3.7: He issued a decree through Nineveh: 'By royal command—no person or animal, no cattle or sheep, shall eat or drink anything.'
3.8: 'Let humans and animals alike wear sackcloth and cry out to God with everything they have. Let every person turn from their wicked ways and from the violence on their hands.'
3.9: 'Who knows—perhaps God will turn and relent, pulling back his fierce anger, and we will not perish.'
3.10: God saw their actions—they had genuinely turned from their evil ways—and he relented. He did not bring the judgment he had announced.

4.1: Jonah was furious. This was the last thing he had wanted.
4.2: He prayed to Yahweh: 'This is exactly what I said back home, Yahweh—why I fled to Tarshish in the first place. I knew who you were: gracious and compassionate, slow to anger, overflowing with loyal love, a God who turns back from disaster. I knew you would do this.'
4.3: 'So take me now, Yahweh. Death is better than watching this.'
4.4: Yahweh answered him: 'Is your anger really justified?'
4.5: Jonah stalked out of the city and sat east of it. He built himself a lean-to for shade and settled in to watch—waiting to see what would happen to Nineveh.
4.6: Yahweh God caused a large leafy plant to grow up over Jonah's head—shelter from the sun, relief from his misery. Jonah was delighted with it.
4.7: But at sunrise the next morning, God sent a worm to gnaw through the plant's stem, and it withered.
4.8: Then God sent a searing east wind, and the sun hammered down on Jonah's bare head until he nearly collapsed. He begged to die: 'Death is better than this life.'
4.9: God asked Jonah: 'Is your anger over the plant really justified?' Jonah snapped back: 'Yes—justified enough to die over it.'
4.10: Then Yahweh said: 'You feel sorry for a plant you never planted, never watered—it sprang up overnight and was gone in a night.'
4.11: 'And should I not care about Nineveh—that great city—with more than a hundred and twenty thousand people who cannot tell right from wrong? And all the animals besides?'
